claim: No significant differences existed in most student perceptions of instructor and course, but students who viewed the syllabus snapshot saw the instructor as less difficult, also indicating they would be more likely to take the course and recommend it to others.
note: Wave 3 (MANOVA). Verified against Ali's Phase 2: reported key F(3,164)=2.14 matches result_01 exactly. Needs a raw-data Bayesian MANOVA/multivariate model.
model_formula: cbind(TBC_COMP, TBC_CARE, percept) ~ snap_syll * snap_inst, Type-III MANOVA with effect-coded factors
contrast_direction: Whether the effect of syllabus snapshot presence differs depending on instructor snapshot presence across the three perception outcomes.
extraction_note: Complete-case N = 170. Cell counts: snap_syll=0, snap_inst=0, n=35; snap_syll=0, snap_inst=1, n=44; snap_syll=1, snap_inst=0, n=46; snap_syll=1, snap_inst=1, n=45. The estimate column stores Wilks' lambda; eta_p2_from_wilks is 1 - Wilks' lambda.

claim: In a retest of items from their first midterm, students showed high performance (70% correct) compared to previous studies, with psychology students (majors and minors) showing better performance than nonpsychology students (81% vs. 69%), d  .74.
note: Verified: reported key t=3.26 matches result_01 exactly. Checked Phase 1 hypothesis column: the article's a priori prediction concerns forgetting-over-time (retest vs original), not the psych-vs-nonpsych comparison actually tested here, so no legitimate pre-specified direction applies -- keep two_sided.
model_formula: 16ItemQuizFollowupPerformance ~ Speciality; Student independent-samples t-test, equal variances
contrast_direction: psychology students minus nonpsychology students; Speciality 1 minus Speciality 0
extraction_note: Recovered group sizes, t, df, exact p, pooled-SD d, mean difference and SE. Psychology n = 23; nonpsychology n = 133; psychology mean = 0.809783; nonpsychology mean = 0.68703; psychology SD = 0.142868; nonpsychology SD = 0.170209.

claim: Sense of belonging was significantly higher in the social annotation condition, particularly for the introductory-level psychology course, and students reported positive perceptions of social annotation.
note: Wave 2 (factorial ANOVA simple effect). Phase 1 hypothesis explicitly predicts belonging would be 'strengthened' by social annotation -- legitimate pre-specified direction, updated from two_sided to positive. Still needs raw-data model fit (2x2 between-subjects design). [Directionality policy: the published significance test for this result is two-sided, so the Bayes factor is computed two-sided to match. The directional Phase 1 hypothesis is recorded but does not change the sidedness of the comparison; only study_20, whose authors ran a genuinely one-sided test, stays one-sided.]
project_verified_result: PSYC 111 simple effect: social annotation > individual annotation; p < .001, eta_p2 = .288
model_formula: BCBSTotalScore ~ Condition * Course; simple effect of Condition within Course == 1 using full-model residual MSE
contrast_direction: PSYC 111 social annotation minus PSYC 111 individual annotation
extraction_note: Recovered simple-effect F, df, exact p, eta_p2, cell sizes, mean difference and SE. Course value for PSYC 111 = 1; individual value = 1; social value = 2; individual n = 14; social n = 12; individual mean = 33.857143; social mean = 42.083333; residual MSE = 27.000149; residual df = 40.

claim: The bias education intervention increased objective and subjective knowledge of bias and multicultural competence, awareness of one's own bias, and behavioral intentions to reduce bias.
note: Phase 1 hypothesis explicitly predicts the intervention 'would increase' knowledge/awareness/intentions -- legitimate pre-specified direction, updated from two_sided to positive. Still Wave 2: Welch (heteroscedastic) independent t-test, needs a raw-data heteroscedastic Bayesian model, not meta.ttestBF()'s equal-variance JZS model. [Directionality policy: the published significance test for this result is two-sided, so the Bayes factor is computed two-sided to match. The directional Phase 1 hypothesis is recorded but does not change the sidedness of the comparison; only study_20, whose authors ran a genuinely one-sided test, stays one-sided.]
model_formula: subj_total ~ condition; Welch independent-samples t-test
contrast_direction: education intervention minus control; condition 1 minus condition 0
extraction_note: Recovered Welch t, Satterthwaite df, group sizes, exact p, mean difference and Welch SE. effect_size_value stores 2*t/sqrt(df), matching the article convention; pooled-SD d = 1.285106. Education n = 195; control n = 303.

claim: Follow-up recognition significantly improved for all pioneer groups.
note: Verified: reported key z=-6.155 matches result_01 (z=-6.154861) exactly. Wave 3: Wilcoxon signed-rank. Needs a rank-likelihood or robust location model, not a t-test conversion.
model_formula: SPSS NPAR TESTS /WILCOXON=womenrec1 WITH womenrec2 (PAIRED)

claim: We found that answering questions about texts (retrieval practice) that were read with and without multitasking, both led to better recall 7 days later than texts read with multitasking followed by rereading..
note: Verified: Ali's own reported key result F(2,116)=4.22 matches the article value stored in reported_result -- and the extraction_note independently confirms the recomputed F(2,116)=10.43 does not recover it under any variant tried. Held.
model_formula: sex-controlled repeated-measures GLM: delayed_recall_percent ~ condition + condition:Sex error term; condition order = RR, MMRP, RP
contrast_direction: delayed recall differs across multitask + reread, multitask + retrieval practice, and no multitask + retrieval practice
extraction_note: The article's Hypothesis 2 delayed-recall condition means are reproduced from the uploaded author data: RR M = 22.02381 (SD = 22.329397), MMRP M = 35.238095 (SD = 23.075031), RP M = 38.333333 (SD = 24.916556). Using all complete cases and controlling for Sex gives F(2, 116) = 10.427106, p = 6.84841e-05, eta_p2 = 0.152383. The unadjusted author-syntax-style repeated-measures GLM gives F(2, 118) = 10.146464, p = 8.58697e-05, eta_p2 = 0.146739. The published inferential value F(2,116) = 4.22, eta_p2 = .07 is not recovered from the uploaded data/syntax variables. The script does not exclude participant 39 and does not force the article value. Variable ordering only changes labels/contrasts, not the omnibus condition F.

claim: Exam performance for those students who opted in was improved beyond what was predicted by their baseline trajectory.
note: Verified: reported key Delta-chi-sq=10.31 matches result_01 exactly. Wave 3: lavaan growth-model chi-square difference test, needs the full two-model Bayesian SEM comparison.
raw_variable_names: opt_in; p_tot1; p_tot2; p_tot3; p_tot4; p_tot5
model_formula: lavaan latent-basis growth model: i =~ 1*p_tot1 + ... + 1*p_tot5; s =~ 0*p_tot1 + m/NA*p_tot2 + 1*p_tot3 + NA*p_tot4 + NA*p_tot5
contrast_direction: Model 7 versus Model 8: intervention-related post-intervention trajectory differences beyond group mean-structure differences
extraction_note: Recomputed from the author's lavaan latent-basis models using p_tot1-p_tot5 and opt_in. Model 7 is lb_fit3: lb_growth_par with group-specific means and post-intervention loadings, under the parallel pre-intervention loading assumption. Model 8 is lb_fit4: lb_growth with group-specific mean structure but loadings constrained equal across opt-in groups. lavaan::lavTestLRT(lb_fit3, lb_fit4) gave Model 7 chi-square = 25.579863, df = 23; Model 8 chi-square = 35.889584, df = 25; chi-square difference = 10.309722, df difference = 2, p = 0.00577128. n_total = 370; opt-out n = 180; opt-in n = 190.
